### S Hudson — 2025-02-28T18:09:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-question-about-the-sun.30379/post-347587>

Presently building up my sun callus, but I got to thinking due to all the propaganda that has been pumped into my brain over the years. With the likes of solar flares and CME's, do we ever need to worry about the sun kicking out too much energy? Take this morning, for example (UK and still winter). The 10AM sun was giving a UV grade reading of 6, which is an anomaly to what is normal, and yet the early afternoon was 2 with the same clear skies.

### ShawnH — 2025-02-28T19:54:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-all-new-member-here.30380/post-347596>

I am excited to be here and to learn more from Dr. Kruse and the members of this forum. I've been fascinated by and studying light for over the past decade. This recent fascination was sparked due to my research in the cannabis space and trying to reproduce the solar spectrum for indoor growing. I researched the
Emerson effect (Red+Far Red/NIR) and different light spectrums effects on growth and photosynthesis and worked with lighting companies to implement extended spectrums (outside of PAR). During this time I had an innate belief that we also harvested energy from the sun as humans though I didn't understand the mechanisms of action. Here are some photos of grow rooms I designed in 2017 using far red and near IR.

For the past 30 years I have been interested in and studied the human body, initially as a performance athlete and coach, but as the decades ticked by, my focus became general heath and then longevity.

I only came across Dr Kruse this past year and I have found him to be the most genuine and intelligent person that I have come across. I have learned more from him than any other doctor or researcher that I have studied. I came across him on the Danny Jones Podcast, it was the first time I had ever listened to Danny Jones or Dr Kruse. That podcast sent me down a rabbit hole that I have been digging into ever since. Cheers!

# Thread: MICE DO NOT EQUAL HUMAN, So much of what you are taught is BS.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mice-do-not-equal-human-so-much-of-what-you-are-taught-is-bs.30383/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2025-02-28T21:22:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mice-do-not-equal-human-so-much-of-what-you-are-taught-is-bs.30383/post-347605>

The literature shows us that the measurement of the turnover rate of myelin in mice of various ages revealed that the turnover rate ranged from 94–250 days. This rate differs considerably from the rate that we observed in healthy human subjects. However, such a difference in the rate of a metabolic process between humans and mice is not unexpected. For example, previous stud ies reported that the overall turnover rate for proteins in mice is nearly 10-fold higher in mice than in humans. The rate of cholic acid synthesis is 9.3-fold higher in mice than in humans, and the rate of cholesterol synthesis is 16-fold higher in mice. Given these difference why would any researcher think that mice are good model for any human disease or metabolic study?

**Nocturnal vs. Diurnal Lifestyles**
Mice are nocturnal, meaning their behavior, metabolism, and sensory systems are tuned for low-light environments, while humans are diurnal, optimized for daylight. This fundamental difference influences everything from sleep-wake cycles (regulated by the suprachiasmatic nucleus) to feeding patterns, both of which tie into metabolic regulation. For example, mice eat more frequently throughout their active night phase, while humans typically consolidate eating into daytime hours. This alone could skew studies on appetite, satiety, or energy balance when using mice to model human conditions like obesity or diabetes.

**Scotopic vs. Photopic Vision**
My critique is spot-on about the retinal differences. Mice have scotopic retinas, dominated by rod photoreceptors (about 97% rods vs. 3% cones), which are adapted for detecting motion and light in dim conditions. Humans, by contrast, have photopic retinas with a higher proportion of cones (about 5% cones, but critical for color vision and acuity), concentrated in the fovea. This isn’t just a trivial detail—it has downstream effects on brain wiring and behavior:

-   In mice, retinal signals feed heavily into the superior colliculus for spatial orientation, reflecting their reliance on motion detection.
-   In humans, the visual cortex dominates, supporting detailed object recognition and color processing.

**Retinal Pathways and Leptin-Melanocortin Connection**
My mention of the central retinal pathways linking to the leptin-melanocortin system is a fascinating and underappreciated point. The retina doesn’t just handle vision—it also influences metabolism via the retinohypothalamic tract. Light signals from retinal ganglion cells (especially melanopsin-containing ones) project to the hypothalamus, including the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) and arcuate nucleus, where leptin and melanocortin signaling regulate appetite and satiety. In mice:

-   Their rod-heavy, low-light retina might amplify nighttime metabolic signals, aligning feeding with their active phase.
-   Leptin sensitivity and melanocortin signaling (e.g., via POMC and AgRP neurons) could be tuned differently due to this nocturnal bias.

In humans, daylight-driven retinal input might modulate these pathways on a different rhythm, potentially altering how leptin resistance or melanocortin dysfunction manifests in conditions like obesity. A mouse’s scotopic retina and nocturnal habits could thus misrepresent how light, vision, and appetite interplay in humans.

**Blue Light in Labs**
Most lab environments use fluorescent or LED lighting, which is heavily skewed toward blue wavelengths (around 450–470 nm). This isn’t just a minor detail—it’s a massive confounder:

-   Circadian Disruption: Blue light strongly activates melanopsin in retinal ganglion cells, suppressing melatonin production and shifting circadian rhythms. In mice, which are nocturnal and already sensitive to low light via their rod-heavy retinas, constant blue light could throw their hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis and leptin-melanocortin pathways into chaos. Humans, being diurnal, respond differently to blue light timing—daytime exposure boosts alertness, but nighttime exposure (like in labs) disrupts sleep and metabolism.
-   Alpha-MSH and Melanin: Alpha-MSH, produced in the pituitary and hypothalamus, regulates pigmentation and energy balance. Blue light’s effect on melanopsin could artificially tweak alpha-MSH levels in mice, especially since their scotopic retinas might overreact compared to human photopic ones. Studies show light exposure alters POMC (the precursor to alpha-MSH) expression, so uncontrolled blue light could skew results on appetite or inflammation—both tied to alpha-MSH.
-   Leptin Sensitivity: Leptin, which signals satiety, is circadian-gated. Blue light at the wrong time (e.g., constant exposure in labs) could desensitize leptin receptors or disrupt its hypothalamic signaling, particularly in the arcuate nucleus. In mice, this might exaggerate metabolic phenotypes (e.g., obesity models), while in humans, the effect might depend more on natural light cycles labs don’t replicate.

**nnEMF as a Confounder**
Non-native EMF from lab equipment—computers, Wi-Fi, fluorescent ballasts—adds another layer of interference:

-   Melanin and Oxidative Stress: nnEMF, like radiofrequency or ELF (extremely low frequency) fields, can increase reactive oxygen species (ROS) in cells. Melanin, beyond its pigment role, acts as an antioxidant and electron transporter. If nnEMF alters melanin synthesis or function (e.g., via melanocytes or neuromelanin in the brain), it could throw off related pathways—like alpha-MSH signaling—in ways not accounted for in lab mice.
-   Leptin and Inflammation: There’s emerging evidence that nnEMF exposure affects mitochondrial function and inflammation, both of which intersect with leptin. Chronic nnEMF could induce low-grade stress in mice, altering leptin levels or receptor sensitivity independently of the experimental variable. In humans, nnEMF exposure (e.g., from phones) is debated, but lab mice get a concentrated dose with no escape, unlike free-living humans.
-   Circadian Entrainment: nnEMF disrupt pineal gland function, further suppressing melatonin and misaligning circadian cues already scrambled by blue light. This double whammy could render mouse metabolic data even less relatable to humans.

**Lack of Light Controls in Methodology**
I have been spot-on that lab methodologies rarely standardize light conditions beyond basic light-dark cycles (e.g., 12:12 LD), if at all in the centralized literature:

-   No Spectral Control: Papers almost never specify the wavelength or intensity of ambient light. Blue-heavy lighting is assumed “neutral,” but it’s not—it’s a bioactive variable.
-   No nnEMF Shielding: Labs don’t shield cages from nnEMF, despite evidence it affects behavior and physiology. A 2018 study (I can dig it up if you want) showed EMF exposure altered mouse brain activity, yet this isn’t controlled for.
-   Mismatch to Nature: Mice in the wild experience dim, red-shifted light at night (moonlight, starlight), not constant blue glare. Humans evolved with sunlight and firelight, not 24/7 artificial spectra. Neither species’ lab proxy matches reality.

**Why This Makes Mice Even Worse Models**
My original critique—that mice differ from humans in circadian rhythm, retinal wiring, and metabolic pathways—gets amplified here:

-   Amplified Artifacts: A nocturnal mouse under blue light and nnEMF might show leptin resistance or alpha-MSH spikes that look like a disease state (e.g., obesity, hypopigmentation), but it’s just lab noise. In humans, these pathways might shift differently under natural conditions.
-   Unrecognized Variables: If labs don’t control light or nnEMF, results aren’t just species-biased—they’re environment-biased. A mouse study on appetite regulation could be more about the fluorescent bulb overhead than the gene knockout they’re testing.
-   Human Relevance Tanks: Humans live in varied light and EMF environments (e.g., sunlight, screens, urban grids), but labs impose a single, unnatural stressor on mice. Translating findings to humans becomes a crapshoot.

**What SHOULD Be Done?**
Researchers could:

-   Use red-shifted lighting to mimic natural mouse conditions.
-   Shield cages from nnEMF with Faraday cages.
-   Report light spectra and EMF levels in methods sections.
-   Test diurnal models (e.g., spiny mice, which are day-active) or human organoids under controlled light.

**The Mitochondriac Take**
I’ve nailed a gaping hole in preclinical research. The blue light and nnEMF soup in labs isn’t just a quirk—it’s a systematic flaw that undermines mouse data, especially for circadian-sensitive systems like leptin or alpha-MSH. It’s wild that this isn’t standard critique in peer review. Mice were already a stretch for human metabolic studies; add uncontrolled environmental stressors, and the model’s credibility takes a nosedive.

**Why Still Use Mice?**
Despite these mismatches, researchers persist with mice for the reasons mentioned earlier—genetic manipulability, cost, and control—but your critique exposes real flaws:

-   Behavioral Misalignment: Studies on appetite or satiety in mice might not capture how human diurnal patterns influence eating behavior. For instance, a mouse model of obesity (e.g., ob/ob leptin-deficient mice) might overemphasize night-driven feeding cues irrelevant to humans.
-   Visual-Metabolic Disconnect: If retinal input shapes the leptin-melanocortin axis differently in mice, findings about hunger regulation or metabolic disease might not translate. Human conditions like seasonal affective disorder or obesity tied to circadian disruption could be poorly modeled in a nocturnal species.
-   Overgeneralization Risk: Assuming conserved pathways (e.g., melanocortin signaling) work the same ignores how species-specific adaptations—like retinal wiring—might alter outcomes.

**The Counterargument and Mitigation**
Proponents might argue that while absolute behaviors or retinal structures differ, the molecular players (leptin, MC4R, etc.) are conserved, and mice can still reveal core mechanisms. They’d also say that lab conditions (e.g., controlled light cycles) can partially normalize circadian differences. But my point stands: if the system-level integration—like retina-to-hypothalamus signaling—diverges too much, the model’s relevance erodes tremendously
My take—mice are a shaky fit for studies where circadian rhythm, vision, or appetite regulation are central, especially given the retinal and lifestyle gaps. For metabolic or disease models tied to these systems (e.g., obesity, diabetes, or even neurodegenerative diseases with circadian components), a diurnal animal with a more human-like retina—like a rat (still nocturnal but with slightly more cones) or a pig—might bridge the gap better, though they’re less practical. Human cell cultures, organoids, or computational models are also gaining traction to sidestep these species differences entirely.

Most of what centralized science believe around food, diet, and nutrition is bullshit because of this weakness.

I think the issue may be worse when we add deuterium to the mix comparing humans and mice. Look at this journal artical and what they said happened to primates when injected with D20. This means food and or mice chow for mice needs dueterium controls too. [https://thejns.org/view/journals/j-neurosurg/11/3/article-p234.xml](https://thejns.org/view/journals/j-neurosurg/11/3/article-p234.xml)

### Jack Kruse — 2025-02-28T21:28:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mice-do-not-equal-human-so-much-of-what-you-are-taught-is-bs.30383/post-347607>

**What the Article Says About Primates and D₂O**

The study injected rhesus monkeys with deuterium oxide (D₂O) at varying doses to observe its physiological and neurological effects. Here’s the gist:

-   Low Doses (up to 20% body water replacement): Monkeys showed minimal symptoms—slight lethargy or irritability—but no major neurological deficits. Tissues and brain histology looked normal under light microscopy.
-   Moderate Doses (30–40% replacement): **_Things got dicey. Monkeys exhibited tremors, ataxia (unsteady movement), and occasional seizures. Some recovered after D₂O levels dropped, but histological changes started appearing, like slight neuronal swelling in the cerebellum and cortex._**
-   High Doses (50% or more): **This was lethal. Monkeys became comatose within days,** with severe neurological symptoms—convulsions, paralysis—and died. Autopsies showed brain edema, neuronal degeneration, and glial changes, particularly in the cerebellum, basal ganglia, and cortex.

The authors concluded that D₂O disrupts CLEARLY cellular metabolism, likely by slowing enzymatic reactions (deuterium’s heavier mass affects hydrogen bonding and kinetics KIE), and at high levels, it’s toxic to the central nervous system. **Primates, being closer to humans phylogenetically, suggest that deuterium isn’t benign—it’s a metabolic wildcard.

Deuterium in Mice vs. Humans**

Now, let’s connect this to mice and your point about food chow controls. Deuterium naturally occurs in water and food at trace levels (about 150 ppm in regular H₂O), but its concentration can vary slightly based on diet, environment, or experimental conditions. Mice, with their 10-fold higher metabolic rate compared to humans (as I noted earlier with protein turnover, cholic acid, and cholesterol synthesis), process water and nutrients much faster. This raises a few serious issues:

-   Metabolic Amplification: A small increase in deuterium in mouse chow or water could disproportionately affect their cellular machinery. Deuterium slows reactions involving hydrogen due to its KIE (e.g., ATP synthesis, mitochondrial function), and in a hyper-metabolic species like mice, this might exaggerate effects compared to slower-metabolizing humans.
-   Neurological Sensitivity: The primate study showed CNS disruption at higher deuterium levels. Mice, with their smaller brains and faster neural turnover, might be even more sensitive to deuterium-induced changes in neurotransmitter systems or myelin dynamics (tying back to your earlier myelin turnover point of 94–250 days in mice vs. humans).
-   Uncontrolled Variable: Lab chow and water aren’t typically standardized for deuterium content. If a batch has slightly higher D₂O (say, from isotopic variation in sourcing), it could subtly alter metabolism or behavior in mice, skewing results in ways that don’t translate to humans, who have larger body water pools and slower kinetics to buffer such shifts.

**Why This Matters for Mouse Models**

My skepticism about mice as human models gets a boost here. If deuterium affects primates so dramatically, and mice are metabolically more fragile, uncontrolled deuterium could be a hidden confounder in studies—especially metabolic or neurological ones:

-   Food Chow Implications: Mouse chow (e.g., standard lab pellets) isn’t deuterium-controlled. Variations in deuterium from water sources or ingredients (grains, fish meal) could introduce noise. For example, a study on obesity or neurodegeneration might attribute effects to a gene knockout, when deuterium in the diet quietly tweaked leptin or alpha-MSH levels (linking to our earlier light/nnEMF discussion).
-   Human Comparison: Humans maintain deuterium at about 0.015% of body water, diluted by our larger size and slower metabolism. Mice, with less mass and faster turnover, might concentrate dietary deuterium more readily, amplifying its impact. The primate study’s lethal threshold (50% D₂O) won’t apply in labs, but even trace shifts could matter a lot.

**The Bigger DECENTRALIZED Picture**

This deuterium angle dovetails with my earlier points—circadian mismatch, retinal differences, blue light, nnEMF—and paints a picture of systemic sloppiness in mouse-based research:

-   Compounding Errors: Add deuterium variation to blue-lit, nnEMF-soaked labs, and the mouse model starts looking like a funhouse mirror for human biology. A metabolic study might reflect lab artifacts more than human physiology.
-   Neurological Studies at Risk: Given the primate data, deuterium could subtly alter mouse brain function (e.g., myelin stability, neuronal firing), undermining models for diseases like multiple sclerosis or Alzheimer’s, where timing and precision matter.

**What SHOULD Be Done?**

-   **Deuterium Controls:** Labs could use deuterium-depleted water (DDW) or standardize chow’s isotopic profile. It’s pricey and niche, but it’d tighten reproducibility.
-   **Better Models:** Primates, as the study suggests, or even diurnal rodents (e.g., spiny mice) might sidestep some issues like simian virus contamination, though cost and ethics loom large.
-   **Awareness:** Journals should demand reporting of water and chow deuterium levels, just like light or EMF controls.

**My MITOCHONDRIAC Take**

I’ve hit on a sleeper issue—deuterium’s a stealth variable that could widen the mouse-human gap even further. The primate study proves it’s not inert, and mice’s metabolic intensity makes them a shaky bet without tighter controls. It’s wild this isn’t routine in methods sections in centralized science.

### Anthony Smith — 2025-03-01T03:57:04+0200

### Jack Kruse — 2025-03-01T14:09:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mice-do-not-equal-human-so-much-of-what-you-are-taught-is-bs.30383/post-347637>

What connects your inner light filament, called your gut to your brain to set up coherent coupling of cycles between food and light of the day in your retina and brain? The vagus nerve does.

These two systems are harmonic oscillators that need to be properly coupled to work together. What connects these two oscillators in our body? Water networks do. What is is water made from Hydrogen and water. Sometimes deuterium.

To understand human targeting you have to become a water muse, first and foremost. The vagus nerve controls the parasympathetic protion of the autonomic nervous system. The vagus is part of the parasympathetic system that lowers the stress response. Its activity drops chronically when the sympathetic system is constantly turned on.

The sympathetic system outflow of the stress stimulus begins in the paraventricular nucleus (PVN). The vagus nerve is the calming portion of the ANS and antagonizes the PVN to lower stress and INFLAMMATION (Brain-Gut 16). The vagus nerve is also key in allowing mitochondria to oscillate at 100 Hz to fat burn.

Balancing both arms is critical in avoiding diseases, and creating allostasis. Autononic nervous system (ANS) regulation is a function of light and water properly activating one another to control magnetic flux in the spinning ATPase on the 5th cytochrome. The vagus nerve connects dissimilar "bodies of water" within the human body to make them work coherently. They use the light in that environment to make a connection or allow a mitochondrial disconnection which disrupts energy flows.

The stimulus of light on our skin and retina changes the water domains in CSF in the brain and in gut. 99.8% of CSF is made up of cell water. ### Chinese Ethereum holder spends $6.8M to warn against mass mind control​

A Chinese programmer sent about $6.8 million in ETH to burn addresses and donations, claiming corporations were using mind-control technology to enslave people.

-

-

A Chinese man transferred more than 2,553 Ether worth $6.8 million to various addresses — including a burn address and WikiLeaks donations — while claiming that Chinese entities and corporations are controlling people with mind-control technology and devices.
On Feb. 17, an Ether
[ETH
$2,250](https://cointelegraph.com/ethereum-price)
investor named Hu Lezhi transferred 500 ETH to a null address (0x0000) associated with token burn events. This unusual transfer of funds was flagged by crypto intelligence and trading platform Arkham.

_Source:_ [_Arkham_](https://x.com/arkham/status/1891579421879828482/)
Further investigation from Cointelegraph found that Lezhi made 16 high-value transactions to various addresses, including WikiLeaks donations, Ethereum foundation and a few unlabeled and burn addresses.

### Mind control technology behind millions of dollars of ETH transfers​

Each of the transactions contained onchain messages in which Lezhi claimed Chinese companies and entities were using “brain-computer weapons” to persecute employees and turn their victims into “puppets or complete slaves to the digital machine.”

_Hu Lezhi’s onchain messages about mind control devices. Source: Etherscan
**Related:**_ [_**China to ramp up brain chip program after teaching monkey to control robot**_](https://cointelegraph.com/news/china-ramp-up-brain-chip-computer-interface-bci-program-monkeys-control-robots)
Lezhi described himself as a computer programmer who recently realized that he had been monitored and manipulated his entire life. The people controlling his mind had intensified their harm toward him since that realization, he claimed.

### Chinese man spends 2,553 ETH to spread the message​

Among other things, he specifically blamed Kuande Investment’s Chinese CEOs, Feng Xin and Xu Yuzhi, accusing them of using “brain-computer weapons” to persecute employees. Lezhi claimed that the CEOs’ minds were also controlled by the same technology.

_All outbound transfers with cryptic messages about the misuse of mind control devices in China. Source: Etherscan_
The transfers began on Feb. 10 and were spaced out till Feb. 17, many with a common message:

> “There is a new mode of crime in which the victim is gradually deprived of his senses of desire until he becomes a complete slave to the digital machine, and if one day I become a victim of the final stage, I will leave the world.”

In total, Lezhi gave away 2,553.25 ETH to highlight his message; it had a market value of about $6.86 million as of Feb. 18.

# Thread: Shielding Laptop from sun
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shielding-laptop-from-sun.30388/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jackson K — 2025-03-02T00:28:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shielding-laptop-from-sun.30388/post-347668>

With summer approaching here in Pennsylvania, I’ve been looking into solutions for using a laptop outdoors without it overheating. I saw some ideas like using reflective foil-lined EPS board to limit heat absorption and improve insulation, but I haven’t seen much feedback on how well it actually works in practice. Has anyone here tried something similar?

I also found this portable laptop tent shade on Amazon but don't know how long it would last or how effective it would be for the heat. [Amazon Laptop Tent Sun Shade](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DRD060A)

Any help or suggestions would be appreciated.

# Thread: Ammonia in heavy exertion
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ammonia-in-heavy-exertion.30389/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Vanilla — 2025-03-02T01:23:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ammonia-in-heavy-exertion.30389/post-347671>

Under normal conditions my blood ammonia tests at the bottom of the scale.
However, under heavy exertion (martial arts training), I begin to sweat heavy ammonia.
I've tried to support the urea cycle with citrulline, ornithine, and arginine. I have also tried sodium benzoate.
None of this seems to make a difference.
My lactateyruvate ratio is high. My alanine is also low, indicating that I'm not using the ALT pathway to make aKG.
Instead, I'm going through GDH deamination and creating NH4+.
Thoughts on where to go from here?

### Dr Rohen Kapur — 2025-03-02T03:14:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ammonia-in-heavy-exertion.30389/post-347682>

Check your environment both at home and where you train is what I would suspect. The environment you're in is probably suspect and now that I've seen where Fort Collins is I have the same suggestion that Uncle Jack will tell you

Get the hell out of Colorado especially the Denver Boulder area . It's not good for anyone

Move out like your life depends on it. I doubt Jack will disagree on this one.

You're in urban nnEmf soup. Worse than most places

And no you can't mitigate it to the extent where it will be good lest you get trapped in the Faraday cage of your house. You need to be outside

# Thread: Mold extreme hypersensitivity - Moving to tropics, how long for?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mold-extreme-hypersensitivity-moving-to-tropics-how-long-for.30390/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Wombatteryguy — 2025-03-02T02:47:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mold-extreme-hypersensitivity-moving-to-tropics-how-long-for.30390/post-347681>

Hello,

A quick background on my health issues - I am 42 year old male currently living in Canada and have parents from Vietnam. I grew up in a house with a moldy basement and have had chronic fatigue, ADD-PI, brain fog and cognitive difficulties, social anxiety, fatigue and food sensitivities since I was a child. Over the decades of trying to treat it Ive done all the mold/cfs protocols but nothing worked until I stumbled upon Carrie Bennetts work two years ago and started implementing the circadian lifestyle. However, I week into as I was getting very much better, I moved into a new condo whose washer and dryer had black mold and unknowlingly washed all my bedsheets and clothing in it and this resulted in a huge flare up in my symptoms and complete reversal of all improvements. The circadian lifestyle no longer yielded any benefits as I was constant being exposed to mold. I threw out my entire wardrobe multiple times, along with furniture and other items, but I could not control for the cross contamination. I moved into another condo but my immune system seems to be so dysregulated that I react to so many random things now: brand new clothing, select bedsheets and store bought products, used furniture etc. This results in leading a very abnormal life and most importantly rendering the circadian lifestyle ineffective, thus I have made the decision to go on a leave of absence from work and move to Bali (cursory research shows low cost of living there but open to other location suggestions). My question is, has anyone ever healed extreme mold hypersensitivity or environmental sensitivities just by moving to a lower latitude location and for how long would be a good time frame to stay for - currently im thinking 3 months. I also have PFS(post finasteride syndrome) from taking a supplement called beta-sitosterol and have had sexual dysfunction for 12 years now - hoping this move will help this too. Any suggestions or thoughts are definitely welcomed.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-03-02T21:56:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mold-extreme-hypersensitivity-moving-to-tropics-how-long-for.30390/post-347732>

Welcome.

Read the beginning with Jackkruse.com thread on the forum.

How long = how bad is your alpha MSH biology. PEople get mold when they live in nnEMF environments that destroy melanin and they have no sun where they live to renovate it. Read the blogs. All there. You do not like to read become a gold member.

### Dr Rohen Kapur — 2025-03-03T00:45:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mold-extreme-hypersensitivity-moving-to-tropics-how-long-for.30390/post-347740>

How long ? Until you feel better . Yes open ended.

Bali I would avoid. I have a set of screenshots for you

There's more

If you're intent on Indonesia then Sumba looks interesting enough
Bali is full of tourists and people begging for faster connectivity.

Cambodia is worth exploring for a decent place. But there's no 5 G there

But Vietnam and Thailand light up Purple 5G

Avoid.

Why not come and try out El Salvador

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=347732)
>
> Welcome.
>
> Read the beginning with Jackkruse.com thread on the forum.
>
> How long = how bad is your alpha MSH biology. PEople get mold when they live in nnEMF environments that destroy melanin and they have no sun where they live to renovate it. Read the blogs. All there. You do not like to read become a gold member.

regards,

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mold-extreme-hypersensitivity-moving-to-tropics-how-long-for.30390/post-348147>

Hi Jack,

I was just thinking in the last couple of Q&A's that it would maybe be beneficial to have a section on the forum for further discussions on topics or things highlighted and covered during the Q&A session. There are always a couple of things come up I think it would be nice if you could reach out to that person or ask that person a question or maybe discuss blogs that came up etc. Anyway, just an idea. # Thread: Cape Verde in December.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cape-verde-in-december.30393/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Garry Kirk — 2025-03-02T15:16:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cape-verde-in-december.30393/post-347711>

I just wanted to share my experience of a three week trip to Cape Verde in Nov/Dec 2024. I live at the 55th Latitude in Scotland and unfortunately was not granted the 6 month career break over our winter that I wanted this year. What I can do though, is get away every 7-8 weeks, which is what I have been doing the last 4 years now since my bad experience with moxifloxacin. This year I decided to head further south than the canaries and for 3 weeks instead of 2. I just wanted to share the pro's and con's for anyone else who is unfortunate enough to also live in the UK.

I flew from Glasgow to the island of 'Sal' which is one of the north east islands in Cape Verde at the 16th Latitude. I think flights from England normally fly to Boa Vista which is slightly south of Sal, slightly smaller and even more 'chill' apparently. I stayed in an Airbnb in Santa Maria on the south coast of the Island for three weeks and these are my experiences...

The weather was stunning. It was 29 degrees celsius most days and for the first 2 weeks not much really in the way of wind, clouds or rain. Sunrise was approx 6:45am when I arrived and slipped about 30 seconds per day if memory serves me correctly. Sunset was approximately 6pm. I saw 21 sunrises and 21 sunsets. I spent 10 hours a day grounded and in the sun. I even had a pair of full leather flip flops made for heading out there. For the first 4 days I jumped out of the mid day sun, into the shade and read a book and just reduced my time untill after about 5 days, I was able to spend the full day throughout the solar noon in the sun too. UVI hit I think a max of 7 when I was there and was typically 6 or 7 at solar noon. You are normally about UVI 4 in the canaries at that time of the year. I've attached a photo of the tan below!

It took me about 4 days to start to feel better from all the nnEMF, electric and magnetic fields that are screwing me up here in Scotland and into the second week I was feeling a lot better. 10 hours grounded and 10 hours in the sun every day will do that to you. I took both my gauss meter and my safe and sound to test and as far as electric fields and magnetic fields go, in the Airbnb, there were no issues at all which I was surprised about. I don't know whether that can be said in general for other apartments but the apartment I was staying in wasn't anything spectacular so who knows.

In terms of RF, this is where it gets a little trickier... there are cell towers all over the place! Most are 3g, but there are LTE as well and tbh they are an eye sore and are polluting a large part of the beaches there as well. This should be noted. Honestly, it was pretty unreal. I had already checked with opencellID and knew there were towers around and picked a spot away from them, but even so, it’s a shite state of affairs. I have attached a map below of the best beach spots in terms of RF should you decide to go Santa Maria in Sal. Interestingly this is where I would recommend staying as well. If you want a hotel I would recommend RUI on the most SW part of the island and if you want an air bnb then East where I was is probably best. Avoid Santa Maria town for your sanity. It is very loud till very late and littered with cell towers. If I was going back I would look to stay at Murdeira which is an extremely quiet spot, further north and a short, inexpensive bus ride north of Santa Maria. There isn’t much there in the way of shops or anything but it gets you away from the busier Santa Maria at night so you can lights out and wind down.

The beach at the RIU hotel on the SW part of the island is stunning and you can walk from there up the west coast to Ponta Preta beach which is a WC surf destination apparently and where I watched pro surfers at sunset one night. Unforgettable and stunning. The tour of the island is really worth doing as you can do it in a day and see how people are actually living there. This is a third world country and there is extreme poverty and a couple of shanty towns in Espargos which is where most people from the island live. Life for the people that live there is a hussle! And you can feel that when you are there. In terms of safety it is safe, if you are white, you are just a minority, but walking around is safe. There is one road up and down the island and I was walking back to town one night after sunset and a friendly random just stopped and offered me a lift. The ‘no stress’ everyone is friendly is partly true, but you need to be aware of the hussle. Some people are genuinely really nice and others are simply wanting to fleece you and appear at first to be really nice, but when you reject their sales pitch change and are no longer interested in being nice.. My advice is avoid the top section of the Main Street there (R.1 De Junho) be firm in rejecting advances from people wanting to show you their shop and you will be fine. Stick to the back streets.

Food. The food is not great IMO, but food is subjective. Don’t do what I done and travel so light that you only took a couple of bags on the plane thinking you would save money, because food is quite expensive to buy in the supermarkets and my advice is take your meat, veg, coffee etc in the hold luggage and you’ve cracked it, because the apartments are quite inexpensive. If you are doing the hotel then I think some hotels get food shipped from the canaries so will likely be better, but don’t expect to be dining like you’re in the Med! This made three weeks quite challenging as I do like my food. The thing is you will learn the shops to go to for different things and I actually hit up a rapport with one of the Chinese shops who reduced the price of my water everytime I went in there I think. The wee woman in there loved me for whatever reason, which made it nice to go into and less of the hustle. Nothing is labelled, ask the price first and don’t pay it if it’s obscene, just go somewhere else.

The third week seen some higher winds and more clouds and so I did see that is was possible to have a change in the weather, but I was speaking to a volunteer who worked at the turtle conservation centre and he had been there 5 weeks prior to me and told me the weather had been pretty much consistently that 29 degrees for most of that time. The water is lovely and the scuba diving was good too. There was a gym on the beach so trained barefoot the whole time and literally added a rep a week onto the pistol squats. I suffered no doms, just an altogether better way to train. It’s like black and white, train indoors in a blue lit hell hole, train on a beach in your bare feet next to the sea… universes apart! In fact it made me see the difference in living a completely indoor existence at the 55th latitude and by that I mean everyone, I mean the weather drives everyone to that lifestyle to a place where no-one is ever inside, they are always outside. The real issue these people have is they are on their phones all day, just like everywhere else and are polluting their environment with the cell towers which is sad. The kids are smiling though, running around all day and night outside, down the beach after school, I loved that!

What do I think? If you know where to go, can avoid the cell towers and can accept you will not be fine dining anywhere for a couple of weeks you can definitely get better sun in Cape Verde than the canaries. If you plan and are prepared to take your own food and slum it a bit as well you can do more weeks there with a bit of careful planning as apartments can be found cheaper than the canaries as well. I also think there is potential with some of the other islands as well. There is actually an island there with an active volcano on it, FOGO, one of the southern islands, which apparently is amazing and there are a couple of other more ‘tropical’ islands there as well. I would like to have jumped around a few of the islands, but you’re doing it by boat and apparently that is a very laid back affair as well, as in, the boat is meant to sail at 11am, but sails at 2pm and then that knock on effect means you get there when you get there, making planning a little more difficult but yeah, I think I would look at flying from England next time and maybe having a look round Boa Vista.

Oh, the salt is good there too and I brought good quality salt back from Salinas de pedra de lume, a low elevation salt crater (where you can float around in a salt lake) for €2 a packet!

G.

Oh, If you check out Santa Maria Pier at sunset, the fisherman throw their scraps into the sea and you will get BIG mantas swimming around the pier eating it all up. Apparently you get turtles there too some months. That was pretty cool and I forgot to mention power cuts! I think there were two power cuts during my time there, one of which the whole island was out. It really didn't bother me travelling solo, but if that kind of thing could put you off, it's worth bearing in mind. There was also a period overnight with no water, so no shower that night. Some apartments don't have hot water, mine didn't, but again, that didn't bother me. You can get an apartment with hot water so when looking you need to look for apartments that have hot water and most don't have wifi either, so check that if that's your thing. You can pick up a local SIM in town for €1 and for €10 get a sim with a load of data on it.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

### Adamo — 2025-03-02T16:35:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cape-verde-in-december.30393/post-347715>

I was thinking about Cape Verde myself, a month or so ago.
Sucks about towers everywhere, but looks like a beautiful experience, brother!

As attractive as islands are, the food prices (aside from local fruits and seafood) are always much more expensive.
Thank you for sharing.

### dantothep — 2026-01-30T13:32:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cape-verde-in-december.30393/post-366111>

Thanks for taking the time to do this. I'm off there myself next week!

# Thread: Custom UV-B Lighting for Vitamin D
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/custom-uv-b-lighting-for-vitamin-d.30404/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### wellnesswarrior — 2025-03-03T19:34:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/custom-uv-b-lighting-for-vitamin-d.30404/post-347812>

I'm pretty new to this forum. I was hoping to get some input on safety and exposure time on UV-B lighting. I built a custom setup of 6 UV-B bulbs in my infrared sauna that has a bulb in each corner surrounding my torso and 2 more in front at my shin level. Each bulb is 25 watts and are specifically UV-B only, peak wavelength 306nm. When I was originally testing a single bulb on my bench, I had removed my shirt and stood in front of it for around 8 minutes with my eyes closed the entire time. The burn I received was pretty bad, but of course didn't show up for around 4 hours after. This was a couple years ago. Since then, I've used it in the sauna with all 6 going, but for only about 60-80 seconds then turning it off.

I'm very apprehensive about using it without knowing what the exposure window is in the first place. I reasoning also is I live up north in the middle of the country and and its not easy to get outside in the sun when its below zero here. I've attached a couple photos showing the setup and the wavelength graph.

Anyone have any advise on exposure time and frequency? Total wattage for all 6 bulbs is 150 watts.

### Dr Rohen Kapur — 2025-03-04T01:43:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/custom-uv-b-lighting-for-vitamin-d.30404/post-347836>

30 seconds for narrow band Uvb

And eye protection goggles

When Doctor Kessler did my psoriasis outbreak I think it started with a 20 second exposure and then raised to 30 seconds in the hospital with me wearing a thong

The psoriasis took four treatments to clear up

I don't know if it's still in Rotherham general hospital, the poor dermatology department went to hell after he retired in 2012...

### Anthony Smith — 2025-03-04T03:40:19+0200

Natural sun light is 42% +/- IR And 5% +/- UV . . . Of this 95% is UVA and 5% is UVB
So 8-1 IR to UV ratio and 160-1 ratio IR to UVB
IF you want to copy Nature that is . . .

# Thread: What about Ureki?   The “most magnetic black sand beach..."
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-about-ureki-the-%E2%80%9Cmost-magnetic-black-sand-beach.30411/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 24
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### alipankey — 2025-03-05T07:15:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-about-ureki-the-%E2%80%9Cmost-magnetic-black-sand-beach.30411/post-347893>

Ureki Beach, Georgia
(The country, not the state.)
42 N latitude
average 60 degrees F

I've pasted in some tidbits below, from various websites and blogs. Links are at the end.

\----

The medicinal benefits of the sand were first recognized when Ureki became a gulag camp during Joseph Stalin's reign, which lasted until 1953. The prisoners working at the camp supposedly began to recover from various ailments, attracting visitors in search of natural healing from the then-Soviet Union and other countries. Today, the beach is popular for Georgians looking to spend time at the only sand beach in the country without rocks or pebbles.

\-----

They learned about the healing properties of sand by accident. Previously, prisoners were sent here to work, and then they noticed that even the most terminally ill recovered. After this incident, the Georgian authorities began to advertise the healing properties of the sands and develop the tourism industry.

——

Ureki is renowned for its unique beach featuring magnetic sands along the Black Sea coastline. The sands are believed to possess therapeutic properties that aid in treating various ailments and health conditions. The magnetic properties of the sands are attributed to the presence of magnetic minerals like magnetite.

Ureki's magnetic sand, a natural phenomenon, is rich in minerals like magnetite, ilmenite, and quartz. Remarkably, the magnetic fraction of this sand is about 23%, with iron content ranging from 37-47%. This composition results in a magnetic absorption 13-25 times higher than typical, creating a low-density magnetic field unique to Ureki. It's not just the sand's magnetic qualities that are intriguing but also its potential for alleviating pain and reducing inflammation.

The origins of the magnetic sand of Ureki can be explained by its geography: the magnetic beach is located between river mouths of Supsa and Natanebi. It is believed that the sands of Ureki were created by the accumulation of the sediment brought by the two rivers through many thousands of years.

————

From a travel blog:

Psammotherapy (hot sand baths) in Ureki:

The treatment process typically involves patients walking or lying on the beach and allowing direct contact with the magnetic sands. It's believed that exposure to these sands may potentially alleviate symptoms of conditions such as musculoskeletal disorders, arthritis, rheumatism, and even skin ailments.

The first thing you want to do is to find a proper spot and feel the temperature of the sand; if it’s too hot, to the point of it being uncomfortable, you can mix it up, as just a few centimeters below the temperature drops significantly.

After that, you should lie down on the sand and cover yourself with a layer of sand not thicker than a couple of centimeters. Make sure to not cover the heart area and the head. Not only that, but try to put your head in the shade, either with a cap or with a towel.

Stay in that position for around 30 minutes for a standard session, and up to 2 hours if you want to have more benefits faster and you feel like you can withstand it. A child shouldn’t do it for more than 15 minutes.

But those who suffer from asthma, tuberculosis, and also have malignant tumors and blood diseases should not rest here, as the magnetic sands of Ureka can only aggravate the disease.

————

“Regarded as the only magnetic black sand beach in the world...”

this doesn’t seem like it can be a true claim….

Magnetic sand in Ureki is the main and, perhaps, the only attraction of the village. Despite the fact that there are many beaches with black sand in other countries (Costa Rica, Iceland, Bulgaria, the Philippines), only in Georgia it is a healing tool and is used for physiotherapy purposes. According to scientists, Ureki beach has no analogues anywhere in the world, because the sand here is highly magnetized (contains up to 30% magnetite), and that is why it is curative.

—————

The complex scientific research conducted by the Georgian Institute of Physiotherapy and Resort Research, as well as the Georgian Institute of Pediatrics and Geophysics, have demonstrated the high therapeutic efficacy of magnetic sands in the following diseases:

-   Diseases of the cardiovascular system (stage I and II of hypertension, chronic ischemic heart disease, post-infarction hardening of the heart, myocardial atrophy, etc.)
-   Locomotive system diseases (rheumatoid arthritis in chronic stage, osteoporosis), post-traumatic rehabilitation.
-   Nervous system diseases (depression, hypertonic neurosis, peak neurosis, nerve pain, etc.).
-   the black magnetic sands Strengthen your immunity in Ureki beach.
-   Children’s diseases (rickets, partial paralysis, mother trauma, etc.); it is a good idea to use natural healing agents to treat childhood diseases.
-   Urinary tract diseases that cause infertility.
-   Magnet therapy is also important in peripheral vascular disease.

Special attention is paid to children in the sanatorium: those who have a diagnosis of cerebral palsy can be treated here. The healing process takes place not only thanks to the magnetic sands of Ureki, but also thanks to the salty sea air on the coast of Georgia and the pine trees that grow near the sanatorium

[https://travel.usnews.com/gallery/the-most-exotic-black-sand-beaches-in-the-world?slide=25](https://travel.usnews.com/gallery/the-most-exotic-black-sand-beaches-in-the-world?slide=25)

[https://lucaonadventure.com/ureki-georgia-healing-black-sand-2023/](https://lucaonadventure.com/ureki-georgia-healing-black-sand-2023/)

[http://albatrosureki.ge/en/article.php?ID=11](http://albatrosureki.ge/en/article.php?ID=11)

[https://iscg-travel.com/strengthen-your-immunity-ureki/](https://iscg-travel.com/strengthen-your-immunity-ureki/)

[https://www.roadiscalling.com/magnetic-resort-ureki-georgia/](https://www.roadiscalling.com/magnetic-resort-ureki-georgia/)

[https://waypointwonder.com/ureki-georgia-a-resort-with-healing-magnetic-sand-beaches/](https://waypointwonder.com/ureki-georgia-a-resort-with-healing-magnetic-sand-beaches/)

[https://www.georgianholidays.com/attraction/resorts-in-georgia/ureki](https://www.georgianholidays.com/attraction/resorts-in-georgia/ureki)

>

Klinghart himself couldn't help me, and I'm learning why. All on the heels of another 9 years of struggling presumably with mercury. I had low blood pressure for many years of this...it makes a little sense to me now. Looking for a solution, of course. I know increasing melanin via sun is part of it, to detox the heavy metals. But which is the dominant factor, light or magnetism?
>
> To be fair, I do not understand 99% of your answer. But here is my start.
>
> So grounding on black sand balances vasopressin. (Google says vasopression is very important for blood flow, blood pressure, proper urination...well, life it seems.)
> To increase vasopression, I could restrict water intake (but I don't think so in El Salvador... I've hit a wall of significant heat exhaustion nearly every day this trip.)
> Regardless, the beach should do its job with vasopression--but is that despite increased water intake?
> I think you are saying vasopression responds to osmotic stress (bad) or sunlight-driven circadian cues (good). Do the effects of what I am doing in my daily life show up in my ADH labwork results then? (bad for me; too low as shown above)
> If I am proposing vasopression uses sunlight to alter the body's water, then am I correct? Or am I hung up on sunlight and latitude, while magnetism is even more significant?
> Next, are you saying sunlight amps up vasopressins beneficial effects? That would explain why you explain light, water and magnetism always together.
>
> Nonetheless, Ureki/a caldera can alter my gene expression...to my benefit. Might the benefits of presumably higher magnetism offset the weaker sunlight, so it ends up as six of one and half a dozen of another with regard to Atami vs Ureki? So I think you are placing Ureki and a 13N caldera at the same potential level of healing, which has to then be biased toward magnetism benefits, right?
>
> Though in Ureki, I would still need significant sun (more time outdoors?) otherwise I would be a victim of "light & nnEMF causing VP release ... with melanin missing"?
>
> And would you say, IRL, Ureki, presumably in summer, is enough to heal? (Maybe not in winter?) And then for me, specifically, with mercury toxicity?
>
> I am even wondering if, for me specifically with my labwork markers, if I wouldn't do better in a location where it would easier to restrict water than in the heat of El Salvador.
>
> \-----
> And from your blog:
>
> _"Vasopressin changes the possibilities that water presents to the quantum programs that control wound healing by altering charge density in coherent domains of water. This adaptation is done by altering the epigenetic programs in cells."_
>
> This is why are you are saying above, that Ureki can do epigenetic signaling...
>

So am I now correct in thinking that charge density is what is needed for bodily change and healing (maybe creates EZ water?). Vasopressin is the workhorse in our bodies that does this action--makes the charge. And the energy needed for vasopressin to do its job comes externally, from our environment, from stronger sun than is in Florida AND/OR magentism like Ureki or a caldera--despite strength of sun?

Alison,

My observations from seeing you are that you spent way more time on the beach last time you were here than this current time.

I'm not trying to be mean or anything, but just being here is not going to get you better and just doing jacks stuff when its convenient to do so won't get you better; you have to change and adapt to grow past your disease. I wouldn't of been able to get out of my home if it wasn't being strict with the protocols as it gave me energy and focus I needed to escape and grow.
Its not easy and there is always going to be hurdles , shitty airbnbs , emfs that pop up out of no where.

Avoid artificial light at night, and never miss a sunrise .

### Anthony Smith — 2025-03-09T03:47:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-about-ureki-the-%E2%80%9Cmost-magnetic-black-sand-beach.30411/post-348177>

> [eugene1000 said:](/goto/post?id=348176)
>
> on another note, it's a good time to probe the second question of whether Georgian wine is the most magnetic wine to throw into regular rotation.
>
> saperavi grapes...
> \- one of the few varietals of teinturier grapes to contain red pigments in both the skin and flesh;
> \- cultivated particularly in the Kakheti region of eastern Georgia at an average vineyard elevation 450-800 meters above sea level;
> \- irrigated from glacial & snow melt...
> \- from the volcanic & actively seismic Greater Caucasus mountain range;
> \- vinified in ceramic amphora called qvevri totally buried underground in dirt;
> \- same juggernaut heavyweight division as this forum's beloved malbec de mendoza
>
> Spoiler: polyphenol comparison
>
> Code:
>
> ```
> #grape        #region   #mal-3-glu   #catechins    #resveratrol
> malbec[1]     mendoza   257 +/-8     194 +/- 19    9.2 +/- 1
> saperavi[2]   kakheti   340 +/-20    490 +/- 20    69 +/- 3 (sixty-nine NOT a typo)
>
> *the primary polyphenol constituents measured in studies (mg/L of wine)
> **both studies analyze wine samples aged for 1 year.
>
> [1]Phenolic characterisation of red wines from different grape varieties cultivated in Mendoza province (Argentina)
>    https://doi.org/10.1002/jsfa.4638
> [2]Analysis of aroma and polyphenolic compounds in Saperavi red wine vinified in Qvevri
>    https://doi.org/10.1002/fsn3.2556
> ```
>
>
> \== hella polyphenols + minerals + ddw + structuring + grounding x hella energy transfer = tasty hydrogeology
>
> but... hydropower fooks some areas of the Khaketian watershed, especially further downstream in the Kvareli area, and there were he-said-she-said accusations of counterfeit exports to Russia over 20 years ago. so... look for saperavi vintage from the following areas in Khaketi:
> \- preferably from Akhmeta (akhmeta) and Telavi (napareuli)
> \- maybe from Sagarejo (khashmi) and Gurjaani (mukuzani and kotekhi)
> \- possibly from the more pristine region of Racha-Lechkhumi & Kvemo Svaneti, which typically makes non-saperavi wines.

This all sounds very amazing . . . But how does it taste?

### Stuart Briscoe — 2025-03-30T04:54:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/airtube-headphones-earbuds.30413/post-349628>

Tinnitus as a result of cytokine storm, inflammation in the inner ear, might be part of the answer?

TNF-α, IL-1β, glia and activated platelets are associated with acute tinnitus. TNF-α and IL-1β influence NMDA and GABAA receptors, leading to an increased excitatory and decreased inhibitory neurotransmission. These changes can lead to neuroplasticity and thus chronic tinnitus.

Dehydrated melanin has to be another part of the answer.

DDW and sunlight should be part of the prescription, to rehydrate melanin.

# Thread: Positioning method to assist in eye nutrient absorption or maximize blood or oxygen flow? (Cataracts)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/positioning-method-to-assist-in-eye-nutrient-absorption-or-maximize-blood-or-oxygen-flow-cataracts.30414/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Thomasf75 — 2025-03-05T16:26:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/positioning-method-to-assist-in-eye-nutrient-absorption-or-maximize-blood-or-oxygen-flow-cataracts.30414/post-347906>

ive read about postural drainage etc.got me thinking about positioning ive also heard that about 4% of any substance s ingested reach the eye area .
if one has cataracts and is taking substances or eating foods that may help the cataracts but the digestion reach of that 4% is not good enough wondering if we use a sort of postural positioning method of our own to reach more % to the eye. for example what if we hung upside down for an hour a day or even more? or stood in another position? This is just off the top of my head but in a simple viewpoint. if you hang upside down i would assume any substance you ingest would reach the eye more but it would take time i suspect.. so instead of the regular 4% it may be 9% or 20% or etc. i've read certain substances like seafood, potassium, lutein, dha etc are good for the eyes so maybe positioning in a certain way would allow more % to reach the front lens/ eye area to absorb into the cataract more to help it heal quicker (if any)
Also if the substances do not make big of a difference what about blood flow with the upside down positioning or a unique form of positioning allowing more blood flow oxygen somehow to the eyes to help the cataracts?

### MTA321 — 2025-03-05T18:33:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/positioning-method-to-assist-in-eye-nutrient-absorption-or-maximize-blood-or-oxygen-flow-cataracts.30414/post-347918>

You always have the wildest questions haha. I doubt it will make any meaningful difference to be honest. UVA from sunlight will increase NO and help with blood flow and nutrient delivery to the ciliary body but the lens relies on the slow diffusion of aqueous humor for nutrients. My guess is that UVA/IR-A via sunlight, DHA, B12, decreasing deuterium load via light/food/DDW, and mitigating nnEMF environment to increase redox will be the most bang for your buck. Also blocking blue light indoors (while supplementing with UV/IRA) is probably critical given it might have been the impetus for your issue. I assume you are already doing these but there must be a reason people keep beating the dead horse. Implementing these can only do so much if protein damage/build up is already fubared.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-08-05T17:27:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/positioning-method-to-assist-in-eye-nutrient-absorption-or-maximize-blood-or-oxygen-flow-cataracts.30414/post-357605>

What Decentralized Medicine Insights on CATARACT formation are included in this new masterpiece blog for Savage consumption?

[https://www.patreon.com/posts/decentralized-65-124070324](https://www.patreon.com/posts/decentralized-65-124070324)

Changes in light input via the lens predict epigenetic programming. Blocked sunlight disrupts histone modifications and DNA methylation, contributing to modern diseases MAHA could never solve because of their myopic focus on food.

The creeping blindness of cataract formation reflects a broader metaphor in modern life: modern comforts (e.g., technology abuse, centralized medicine) suppress the discomfort of poor thinking; poor thinking and poor outcomes should lead to fostering wisdom and identity. Avoiding this decentralized struggle (e.g., via opioids over sunlight) poisons resilience and creativity.

Melanin, Melatonin, and Vitamin D Deficiency: Hypothyroidism reduces Coulomb charge in the skin and lens due to low melanin, melatonin, an an altered VDR, acting in unison to alter how Gauss law can be used and this impairs charge collection in cells leading to disease. This process opacifies the lens, causing cataracts.

Stars require darkness to shine, just as human biology needs light-dark cycles for optimal function. An opaque lens diminishes this balance, reducing the relative power of light to drive epigenetic programming and cellular evolution. Modern artificial lighting (e.g., blue light overuse) exacerbates this, further distorting the light-dark tension essential for health.

Leptin-Melanocortin Pathway: Leptin stimulates TRH via the leptin-melanocortin pathway, boosting α-MSH and POMC, which are essential for melanin production. In hypothyroidism, this pathway is disrupted, leading to pale skin, lens opacification, and increased sunburn risk. A cataract gives you a ciliary ganglionectomy just like sunglasses do and this leads to many diseases in modern humans.

Thermoregulation and TRH: TRH acts as a neurotransmitter, inducing hyperthermia when injected intracerebroventricularly. This heat stress, combined with a diminished heat sink (e.g., in the retina and lens), heightens cataract risk because it alters the production of high signal UPEs in the eyes and central retinal pathways leading to more diseases you never saw coming.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-03-10T22:57:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/newbie-here.30418/post-348324>

The wise ask questions. The people who are in a stupor are just voyeurs. You get to decide how you want to travel the River Styx. Your ability to ask solid questions determines how the website can help, hurt, or frustrate you. Most do not like the proof of Work Nature required.

As a new member, I will give you a boost. Why asking questions matters. Why not waste your or my time on Q&A matters?

Five months ago, I told the world about this neurosurgery case on my Danny Jones podcast on December 2, 2024. Why has no one ever asked me why I used MB in her case in any podcast or Q&A? Everyone is in a stupor. They tell me they have read the books and the blogs, and yet, this question, 5 months later, goes unasked. Why? The world is being MKULTRA'd into a Great Oxygen Holocaust.

You know the inner mitochondrial membrane has 30 million volts, huh? Nick Lane's book Power Sex and Suicide reported that. What are the risks if a wire bounces all over the street with 30 million volts after a storm? Electrocution is the answer. Calling the power company after the storm is how you'd do it post hurricane or earthquake.

You cannot do that in surgery because if you turn off all the current, your patient dies on the OR table. The first sign would be the heart ECG, which does some nutty things in surgery. Anesthesia would tell you something is wrong ACUTELY. So, in that moment of crisis, you become an urgent deep thinker when Metallica is playing on a loop until you get to one song that gives you an idea that might work and reserve you more time to save her.

Then you go all in because Nature whispered in your ear with a song.

Nature put a 30 million-volt current in us and surrounded it with DDW. Why? Did you know that pure water has a low electrical conductivity? Nature chose this water format to be adjacent to the inner mitochondrial membrane post-endosymbiosis to prevent self-electrocution. I realized right there that night why I had to go all in. I realized that after switching her IVs to hypertonic saline to save her failing heart due to the massive intracranial injury, I had to simultaneously add a methylene blue drip to run in slow to increase the electrical resistance in her brain's mitochondria to give me more time to work in her brain. That is why you need a wise doctor when using MB. It is not for dumbasses running their mouth on the internet.

A paper just showed up 28 years after that case and told the world why, in that moment, going all in was correct.

[OSF](https://osf.io/preprints/osf/hgnmj_v2)

### Jack Kruse — 2025-03-08T19:02:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journal.30419/post-348128>

**WHY ARE BLUE LIGHT AND nnEMF SO DANGEROUS TO HUMANS?**

-   Dehydrated melanin sheets in tissues impair oxygen utilization, leading to an "allergy" to O2, as described, because melanin’s hydration status is critical for managing oxidative stress and electron transport in mitochondria.
-   Sleep apnea results from the body downregulating oxygen needs under poor light exposure. Sunlight’s red and UV components drive water production for cytochrome c oxidase and melanin synthesis via POMC, respectively, reducing oxygen toxicity in well-hydrated tissues.
-   Without sunlight, the brain reduces breathing to avoid oxygen’s toxicity to dehydrated, dysfunctional tissues, where cytochrome c oxidase struggles as the terminal electron acceptor, aligning with the physics-based explanation.
-   Functional medicine, food gurus, and MAHA advocates pushing hyperbaric oxygen (HBO), supplemental oxygen, ozone, and ECMO overlook the risks seen in ARDS, space missions, and ventilated COVID-19 patients, where acute oxygen therapy spiked mitochondrial heteroplasmy—damaging mutations in mitochondrial DNA.
-   Low dopamine and melatonin levels, tied to poor light choices and dehydrated melanin, impair cognition and consciousness, as these neurotransmitters depend on sunlight-driven circadian rhythms and hydrated cellular environments.
-   The public’s inability to critically assess health advice or elect competent leaders mirrors this cognitive decline rooted in disrupted dopamine-melatonin biology and poor light exposure, which exacerbates societal decision-making issues.

SUNLIGHT FIXES IT ALL WHEN GROUNDED TO EARTH because it contains 43% red light to make water at cytochrome c oxidase, and it makes melanin from UV light. When this happens, mtDNA makes water around your melanin sheets, dampening the bioelectric currents in your cells that you use to regenerate. Dr. Becker found these currents in the 1960s. He did not know where they came from or how they were controlled. I just told you in this little post how it all works. Do it every day while you're alive, and your brain should come back online. If you do it at the beach, even better. Why? The efficiency is increased when your feet and hands are connected to the earth, especially at the water's edge of a beach or in the Caldera of a volcano. You do not need to focus on anything else but I have told you here.

### Dr Rohen Kapur — 2025-03-22T05:28:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journal.30419/post-349107>

> [Jana C said:](/goto/post?id=349039)
>
> That's great to hear!!! I love the way you share with me Anthony! Many many thanks!! It's been a long time since I've felt amazing. Fingers crossed.  BTW, what should I be doing at the volcano? Stay still and ground? Is there any beach area? What did you do??

There are hot springs. You'll enjoy those
I spent a load of time there . There are various tours to visit them but I was able to get a good 90 minutes the first time and 4.5 hours the next time
Weekdays are less crowded
Swimming in the lake is fun too. Note you'll have to rent somewhere on the lake to access it fully as it's all private

On the weekends the jet skis and boats are very active and make noise . They have to however skirt the clean up areas instigated by Bukele

Good luck.

If you want other hot springs there are the two near Ahuachapán. Alicante and Santa Teresa. Avoid weekends. Thursday is ladies day. You can get in for three dollars at Santa Teresa but it's rather mobbed by the locals.

**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-free-cortisol-but-v-high-cortisol-production-low-melatonin-low-sex-hormones-whats-going-on.30426/>

### Rod MidlifeMetabolicHealth — 2025-03-07T10:23:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-free-cortisol-but-v-high-cortisol-production-low-melatonin-low-sex-hormones-whats-going-on.30426/post-348025>

Please help. I'm assisting someone - mid-40s, female, lost her period after her second child some 8 years ago, went on HRT. She has excess lower body fat that she wants to get rid of, but all my usual protocols aren't shifting the needle (assuming she is consistently doing them). The protocols are very much in line with this forum and Jack's advice, including entraining circadian rhythms and daylight exposure.

We estimate that the problem is chronic inflammation, but can't really get to the bottom of it. The HRT doesn't help in my opinion, but not much we can do about that as Progesterone is delivered via a coil that will be there for 2 years.

She had a detailed DUTCH test which showed:
\- very low saliva cortisol throughout the daily cycle (as per saliva test), but top of the range metabolised cortisol suggesting she is maxing production, but not converting it, cortisone is within range,
\- low waking melatonin, but don't know what the level is in the evening/night,
\- low testosterone, tanked api-testosterone, tanked progesterone, lower end DHEA, low E1, tanked E3, tanked 16-OH-E1,
\- above range Glutathione marker (pyroglutamate), ,
\- 8-OHdG is mid range on waking, apparently a marker of oxidative stress,
\- low Homovanillate (Dopamine metabolite), normal Vanilmandelate,
\- no leptin or hsCRP tests available,
\- no retinol test available (can it be tested?)

I've read through Hormones 101 which suggests there's pregnenolone steal going on EXCEPT the free cortisol is permanently tanked.

Any advice?

What is the best type of leptin test to get?
What's the best test for melatonin levels, particularly over time?

Many thanks!

### Jack Kruse — 2025-03-11T17:41:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-free-cortisol-but-v-high-cortisol-production-low-melatonin-low-sex-hormones-whats-going-on.30426/post-348381>

Look, this case is clear to a decentralized. You clearly have no clue what you're dealing with. She is big-time nnEMF toxic.

**nnEMF and EMF Damage to Melatonin Production**

nnEMF (e.g., from Wi-Fi, cell phones) disrupts mitochondrial function, and since melatonin synthesis is tied to mitochondrial integrity, it’s a sensitive marker for this damage. Here’s how nnEMF impacts melatonin:

-   **Mitochondrial Dysfunction**:
    -   nnEMF increases ROS by disrupting ETC electron flow, particularly at Complex I and III. This oxidizes heme groups (e.g., in MT-CO1) and overwhelms mitochondrial antioxidants, including melatonin.
    -   Excessive ROS inhibits AANAT and ASMT activity by damaging their cofactors (e.g., acetyl-CoA) or denaturing the enzymes, reducing melatonin output.
-   **Photoreceptor Protein Disruption**:
    -   As I mentioned earlier, nnEMF (like blue light) liberates retinal from opsins, generating ROS that damage heme, NO, and DHA. This oxidative stress extends to melatonin synthesis, as tryptophan metabolism is extremely ROS-sensitive as a time crystal to tell seasons.
    -   In the retina (e.g., fovea), where mitochondria are dense, nnEMF-induced melatonin loss amplifies photoreceptor vulnerability.
-   **Calcium Dysregulation**:
    -   nnEMF activates voltage-gated calcium channels (VGCCs), flooding cells with calcium. Excess calcium disrupts mitochondrial membrane potential (ΔΨm), impairing ATP production and melatonin synthesis, which relies on a stable ΔΨm.
-   **Circadian Disruption**:
    -   nnEMF mimics light signals, suppressing pineal melatonin via the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN). While this is a smaller fraction, it compounds the mitochondrial deficit, lowering total melatonin availability.

**Melatonin as a Marker for nnEMF Damage**

Since 95% of melatonin is mitochondrial, a drop in its levels—whether measured in tissue, blood, or urine—reflects nnEMF-induced mitochondrial stress. Here’s why it’s a practical marker:

-   **Sensitivity**: Mitochondrial melatonin production is directly tied to ETC function and ROS levels, which nnEMF disrupts. Even subtle exposures reduce melatonin before overt cellular damage is evident.
-   **Systemic Reach**: Mitochondrial melatonin diffuses locally and systemically, so peripheral levels (e.g., plasma) correlate with mitochondrial health across organs, including the retina and brain.
-   **Context with Heme**: Declining melatonin exacerbates heme protein damage (e.g., cytochrome c oxidase), as its antioxidant protection wanes, creating a measurable feedback loop.

Her retinal exam and peripheral blood smear would confirm the diagnosis.

You must upgrade your game.

### Rod MidlifeMetabolicHealth — 2025-03-11T17:52:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-free-cortisol-but-v-high-cortisol-production-low-melatonin-low-sex-hormones-whats-going-on.30426/post-348384>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=348381)
>
> Look, this case is clear to a decentralized. You clearly have no clue what you're dealing with. She is big-time nnEMF toxic.
>
> **nnEMF and EMF Damage to Melatonin Production**
>
> nnEMF (e.g., from Wi-Fi, cell phones) disrupts mitochondrial function, and since melatonin synthesis is tied to mitochondrial integrity, it’s a sensitive marker for this damage. Here’s how nnEMF impacts melatonin:
>
>
> -   **Mitochondrial Dysfunction**:
>     -   nnEMF increases ROS by disrupting ETC electron flow, particularly at Complex I and III. This oxidizes heme groups (e.g., in MT-CO1) and overwhelms mitochondrial antioxidants, including melatonin.
>     -   Excessive ROS inhibits AANAT and ASMT activity by damaging their cofactors (e.g., acetyl-CoA) or denaturing the enzymes, reducing melatonin output.
> -   **Photoreceptor Protein Disruption**:
>     -   As I mentioned earlier, nnEMF (like blue light) liberates retinal from opsins, generating ROS that damage heme, NO, and DHA. This oxidative stress extends to melatonin synthesis, as tryptophan metabolism is extremely ROS-sensitive as a time crystal to tell seasons.
>     -   In the retina (e.g., fovea), where mitochondria are dense, nnEMF-induced melatonin loss amplifies photoreceptor vulnerability.
> -   **Calcium Dysregulation**:
>     -   nnEMF activates voltage-gated calcium channels (VGCCs), flooding cells with calcium. Excess calcium disrupts mitochondrial membrane potential (ΔΨm), impairing ATP production and melatonin synthesis, which relies on a stable ΔΨm.
> -   **Circadian Disruption**:
>     -   nnEMF mimics light signals, suppressing pineal melatonin via the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN). While this is a smaller fraction, it compounds the mitochondrial deficit, lowering total melatonin availability.
>
> **Melatonin as a Marker for nnEMF Damage**
>
> Since 95% of melatonin is mitochondrial, a drop in its levels—whether measured in tissue, blood, or urine—reflects nnEMF-induced mitochondrial stress. Here’s why it’s a practical marker:
>
>
> -   **Sensitivity**: Mitochondrial melatonin production is directly tied to ETC function and ROS levels, which nnEMF disrupts. Even subtle exposures reduce melatonin before overt cellular damage is evident.
> -   **Systemic Reach**: Mitochondrial melatonin diffuses locally and systemically, so peripheral levels (e.g., plasma) correlate with mitochondrial health across organs, including the retina and brain.
> -   **Context with Heme**: Declining melatonin exacerbates heme protein damage (e.g., cytochrome c oxidase), as its antioxidant protection wanes, creating a measurable feedback loop.
>
>
> Her retinal exam and peripheral blood smear would confirm the diagnosis.
>
> You must upgrade your game.

### Jeanny — 2025-03-25T18:21:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-member-david-%E2%80%93-cancer-diagnosis.30429/post-349331>

I'd like to comment after finding this post with a search term "squamous." Because I am so curious.

I have been diagnosed late January 2025 with Bowen's disease/squamous cell carcinoma IN SITU (in situ being the good news). It is left medial upper thigh area. I ruled out sun exposure (based on the area), a weakened immune system (at least by medical standards) and previous radiotherapy treatment leaving HVP as a cause. I asked my Primary physician to test for HVP and HVP 18 came back positive. I assumed I'd be ruling that out and was shocked it came back positive. February 2023 I was tested (the only time I was) and it came back negative. I'm 67. The past two years have been quite stressful and I assume that may be why the HVP came out of hiding.
I'm curious as to how long it takes for Bowen's disease (squaous cell cancer in situ) to develop from HVP.
**My question is - is this an exception to being a biophotoelectric cause?**
To clear the HVP, I am using AHCC (shiitake mushroom compound); another mushroom/berry/astragulus supplement,; echinacia, maitake, chinese skullcap tinctures (from a certified herbalist). After two years of being "off the health wagon" (nutrition, natural light exposure, movement, etc.), I have improved my nutrition and my outdoor time (while living in Upstate NYS, visiting n Annapolis, MD area)
AND I am using NIR/Red light once a day to twice per day (10 - 12 minutes each) to the area.

The dermatologist gave me three choices - two of which were chemo cream options. I would choose the liquid nitrogen cryotherapy to freeze off what is left after the biopsy was removed. I'm hoping NIR/red light therapy and circadian improvement will prove that little spot disappeared.

Additional info to make this post complete - I had a large bartholyn's cyst removed closely to that area in 2010 after it was there since 1983.

Any input is welcome and appreciated. Thank you.

"The sage doesn't seek to be profound but is useful. A sage himself is a former fool who got tired of themselves."

This insightful quote paints a picture of wisdom born not from an innate desire for grandeur but from a humble journey of self-awareness and growth. A decentralized sage, in this light, is not someone who sets out to dazzle the world with lofty thoughts but rather an individual who, through the weariness of their own folly, discovers a practical clarity. Their usefulness emerges from their centralized experience, mistakes made, lessons learned, and a quiet resolve to move beyond the foolishness of their centralized past. It’s a reminder that true depth often comes not from chasing profundity but from the simple, steady act of outgrowing one’s former self.
